nodes.label: Current Environment and Pain Points Slide

provenance.quote:
CURRENT ENVIRONMENT & PAIN POINTS
Medbrief is building a platform to support medical-legal case intelligence by comparing patient case documentation against NICE medical guidelines. Today, this is a highly manual process involving legal and clinical review of large volumes of PDF documentation, requiring complex interpretation and evidence review.
KEY CHALLENGES
Large volumes of unstructured medical-legal PDF documentation.
Manual comparison between case documentation and NICE guidelines.
Need to identify correct historical NICE versions based on case dates.
Sensitive medical and personal information risks.
Entity ambiguity across patients, doctors, hospitals, and documents.
OCR limitations and inconsistent document quality.
Need for explainable, auditable, and defensible outputs.

nodes.statement: Medbrief already has the compliance certifications that NICE requires to grant API key access, which means Medbrief may be able to obtain the key faster than a typical applicant.

provenance.quote: you guys already have the compliance certifications that they ask for to get the API key, so you might get it faster.

nodes.label: Every medical-legal case will contain handwritten notes in some form
nodes.statement: Every case, without exception, will have handwritten notes of some kind — Lloyd George envelopes, nursing notes, or handwritten observation charts. Even trusts now using digital systems still have older handwritten records.

provenance.quote: Every case, every case will have handwritten notes of some way, shape or form, every single one.

nodes.label: A typical case references one but sometimes two or three NICE guidelines
nodes.statement: Normally a case references one guideline, but it can quite often be two or three, particularly where the patient has multiple conditions (e.g. obstetrics plus diabetes). Simpler cases such as a fall in hospital would reference one guideline.

provenance.quote: I probably say, normally, no, no. It's one, but you can quite often be two or three... It depends on the case.

nodes.label: NICE previously directed users to National Archive for old guideline versions
nodes.statement: Approximately two years before this meeting, NICE told Steve Ashford that the only way to retrieve old guideline versions was through the National Archive. NICE said they were looking into making their own archive accessible but had not confirmed it at that time.

provenance.quote: about two years ago I asked them whether we could get old versions of guidelines and they said the only way to get them was to go to the National Archive, you see. So, but they did say they were going to look into whether they could make their archive available.

nodes.label: Medbrief's existing Document Intelligence OCR output can be reused by Kumulus
nodes.statement: Medbrief already runs OCR on handwritten notes using Document Intelligence. Kumulus can reuse this OCR output rather than performing OCR from scratch, which remains out of scope.

provenance.quote: are you guys already doing OCR on the hand reading? If so, can we use this OCR on our project? Yes. Okay, okay, that helps a lot.

nodes.label: Cases must be compared against the NICE guideline version current at the time of the clinical event
nodes.statement: If a case dates from four years ago, the comparison must use the NICE guideline version that was in force at that time — not the current version. The NICE website only shows the latest version; older versions must be retrieved separately.

provenance.quote: if somebody had a case that dated back four years ago, when it happened, you would need to look at the guidelines associated with that condition at that time. Now, if the guidelines have been updated, the NICE website will only show you the latest version of the guidelines.

nodes.label: Sending PII to LLMs is too risky even within Azure AI Foundry
nodes.statement: Even when using Azure AI Foundry/Azure OpenAI models within the Azure boundary, sending patient PII to LLMs is considered a compliance breach and too risky; all PII must be pseudonymised before LLM calls.

provenance.quote: Even when using Azure Foundry models, it's too risky. We don't want to share PII information that's a breach of compliance.

nodes.label: Databricks with Spark is best-in-class for parallel processing of unstructured medical-legal data
nodes.statement: For workloads with large volumes of unstructured data requiring parallel processing, Databricks with Apache Spark is the best-in-class choice; Microsoft Fabric would be slower and less suitable.

provenance.quote: Databricks is the best to win the market for doing parallel processing using Spark, so if you want a solution that's fast and reliable. That's Databricks.

nodes.label: Medbrief already has clinician-reviewed ground truth for sample cases
nodes.statement: For the sample cases to be used in the PoV, a clinician has already reviewed them and the breach determinations are known, providing ready-made ground truth for evaluation of the comparison layer.

provenance.quote: the cases that we are going to send over to you, a clinician has already gone through them. So we already know which reaches were there. So that's a way that we could use, like as a ground truth

nodes.label: Entity dimensions must be scoped per-case to prevent cross-case identity confusion
nodes.statement: The lookup table dimensions (patient, doctor, etc.) must be separated per case to prevent accidentally linking an entity from one case to a different case.

provenance.quote: we are tying up per case. So in this way we don't get lost or the challenge is to not create confusion.

nodes.label: Engagement estimated at 10-12 weeks
nodes.statement: 10 weeks for delivery activities plus 2 weeks for testing and validation, totalling approximately 12 weeks. This is larger than the standard 6-8 week POV.

provenance.quote: We are estimating 10 weeks. attribute everything and two weeks for testing and validation and final tweaks. So that's a three months project we are looking here.

nodes.label: Microsoft funding deadline is 12 weeks from project start
nodes.statement: Microsoft requires the POV solution to be delivered within 12 weeks of start. Exceeding this deadline risks losing the Microsoft incentive funding entirely.

provenance.quote: Microsoft funding is related to timing of completion of the project. So if we block on some milestone or we delay a lot the project, we are at a high risk of losing the Microsoft incentives.

nodes.label: Azure cost estimate capped at approximately $700/month including 30% buffer
nodes.statement: Kumulus's Azure cost calculator shows an estimated maximum of approximately $700 per month for the MVP, inclusive of a 30% contingency buffer. John expects actual spend to be lower. Databricks is the dominant cost component.

provenance.quote: that's a 30% buffer here, okay, that I always put. I don't think we are going to leverage $700 a month for this MVP.

nodes.label: Full service cost is approximately £35,000 before Microsoft incentives
nodes.statement: The total undiscounted price for Kumulus's proposed engagement is approximately £35,000.

provenance.quote: the total price of service cost is thirty-five.

nodes.label: Microsoft funding reduces cost by approximately £22,000
nodes.statement: Kumulus is applying two Microsoft incentive funding streams (Data POV and AI POV) which together reduce the engagement cost by approximately £22,000.

provenance.quote: We were able to reduce that for almost 22, 000 pounds using Microsoft funding.

nodes.label: Net cost to Medbrief after Microsoft incentives is £13,000
nodes.statement: After applying both the Data POV and AI POV Microsoft incentive streams, the net cost to Medbrief for the full in-scope engagement is £13,000.

provenance.quote: with that discount, the end price is 13,000 pounds, with everything is in scope.

nodes.label: Five Formal Project Objectives (verbatim)
nodes.text: João stated five named project objectives: (1) validate the NICE integration with the API; (2) improve the case data processing and extraction; (3) resolve entities; (4) prove secure AI usage; (5) demonstrate case versus guideline comparison.

provenance.quote: So these are the five main objectives. Validate the needs integration with the API, improve the case data processing, extraction, resolve entities, prove secure AI usage, demonstrate cases versus guideline comparison.

nodes.label: System will not provide clinical or legal conclusions — only guideline alignment or deviation
nodes.statement: The system's output is limited to stating whether a procedure followed or deviated from the guideline and why. No clinical or legal verdict will be provided. Kumulus explicitly stated they are not doctors or judges; the tool provides evidence for human decision-makers, not the decision itself.

provenance.quote: No clinical or legal conclusions will be provided. We are going to say if the case we're following, the guideline or not... it's not our work to say we are not, we are not doctors or, you know, judge. So we are going to provide all the tools and necessary information to take a decision and understand what happened, but it's not in our scope to say if it is, you know, right or no.

nodes.label: Model fine-tuning is explicitly out of scope; pre-trained Foundry/Azure OpenAI models will be used as-is
nodes.statement: LLM fine-tuning is out of scope for the MVP. Kumulus will use Azure AI Foundry or Azure OpenAI APIs with prompt engineering only. Fine-tuning could be explored in a future phase if results are insufficient.

provenance.quote: AI and reasoning scope, so model fine tuning. The model fine tuning is out of scope as we are going to use Azure AI Foundry APIs or Azure Open AI APIs. We are not fine tuning the model right now. This could be explored in the future if needed.

nodes.label: Entity dimension schema: five named types — patient, doctor, hospital, case, document
nodes.text: João specified the initial entity dimension schema by name: patient, doctor, hospital, case, and document. These five are the baseline types for the PoV; additional types can be added in future phases.

provenance.quote: We are going to create the dimensions that I told you last time, so patient, doctor, hospital, case, and document for now. In the future, we can expand

nodes.label: Anonymisation compliance certification is out of scope for the PoV
nodes.statement: Obtaining formal compliance certification for the anonymisation component is explicitly out of scope. The engagement builds an MVP implementation of pseudonymisation, not a production-certified solution.

provenance.quote: NCN anonymization, compliance certification is out of scope. Of course, we're just doing an MVP. We're not creating a solution to get a certification. That's very important.

nodes.label: PoV NICE API integration enables future full-catalog ingestion with no new integration work
nodes.statement: Although only the guidelines relevant to the selected cases will be ingested during the PoV, the same API integration can be used to ingest the full NICE catalog in the future without any new integration development.

provenance.quote: the full catalog ingestion is not in scope for this first phase, but you are going to be able to ingest all the needs catalog if you want with the API integration we are going to connect. We are just bringing what's necessary for the use case, but it's the same API.

nodes.label: Fabric is an acceptable choice only if slow batch processing is tolerable; Databricks is required for responsive parallel workloads
nodes.statement: João drew an explicit performance trade-off: if Medbrief is willing to run the pipeline and wait an hour for results, an alternative tool to Databricks could be considered. For a solution that needs to be fast and reliable under production load, Databricks is the only recommendation.

provenance.quote: If you want a solution that you can run the process and wait for one hour and get done, maybe we can take a look in other tools. But for me, that's the best in class for your use case in your scenario.

nodes.label: Introductions and Screen Share
nodes.summary: SPEAKER_02 (Medbrief) opens the meeting, introduces John from Kumulus, and invites him to present. John shares his screen after a brief technical fumble.

nodes.label: Proposal Overview and Context Recap
nodes.summary: John recaps the proposal scope: gathering NICE guideline data, comparing it with medical-legal case documents, data anonymisation, and data tokenisation. He outlines what the presentation will cover.

nodes.label: In-Scope and Out-of-Scope Definition
nodes.summary: John walks through the fixed-fee scope boundaries: one medical domain, one or two real cases, entity extraction, anonymisation, and an initial case-vs-guideline comparison. Out-of-scope items include handwritten note OCR from scratch, full historical NICE processing, and production application development.

nodes.label: Q&A: Guidelines, Handwritten Notes, and NICE API Access
nodes.summary: Laura (Medbrief) asks about multiple NICE guideline references per case; Steve clarifies that cases typically reference one to three guidelines, rarely more. The group also discusses handwritten notes (present in every case) and confirms Medbrief's existing OCR can be reused. Laura asks about deterministic pseudonymisation and NICE API key access.

nodes.label: NICE API Historical Guidelines and Step 1: Data Ingestion
nodes.summary: John explains that the NICE API stores historical guideline versions queryable by year and ID, reducing the need for Kumulus to maintain its own archive. Steve flags a two-year-old concern about archive availability that may now be resolved. John describes Step 1 (NICE API) and Step 2 (case PDF ingestion), including chunking and metadata strategy.

nodes.label: Entity Extraction, Identity Resolution, and Pseudonymisation
nodes.summary: John covers Steps 3 and 4: detecting and classifying entities (patient, doctor, hospital) across documents using similarity matching, and replacing PII with deterministic tokens before sending data to LLMs. Brandon raises the need to agree an entity classification schema with Medbrief's data science team before coding begins.

nodes.label: Full Architecture Walkthrough: Data Factory, Databricks, and AI Layer
nodes.summary: John presents the end-to-end Azure architecture: BLOB storage, Data Factory for orchestration, Databricks for raw/silver/gold layer processing, Azure AI Foundry for LLM inference, and Azure Monitor for observability. He explains why Databricks is preferred over Microsoft Fabric for this unstructured, parallel-processing workload.

nodes.label: Case-vs-Guideline Comparison Logic and Evaluation Strategy
nodes.summary: John walks through the deviation-check logic: selecting a case, retrieving the matching NICE guideline version, cross-referencing clinical events, and producing an evidence pack with page-level traceability. Laura notes that clinician-reviewed ground truth already exists for the sample cases; Momoko (Medbrief) raises the challenge of evaluating pseudonymisation on handwritten content that OCR may have missed.

nodes.label: Project Activities, Success Criteria, and Delivery Approach
nodes.summary: John summarises the six delivery steps (scope alignment, kickoff, data processing, entity extraction, comparison, handover), outlines the WBS and Gantt approach, and reviews success criteria covering NICE API access, entity extraction, anonymisation, comparison quality, and explainability. Laura requests quantitative evaluation metrics rather than manual review.

nodes.label: Microsoft Funding, Timeline, and Milestone Structure
nodes.summary: John presents the 10-to-12-week timeline (two weeks for testing), framing the engagement as an MVP rather than a standard POV. He stresses the 12-week Microsoft funding deadline and confirms that Medbrief can take full ownership of deliverables at the end of milestone one without committing to a phase two.

nodes.label: Azure Cost Estimates and Assumptions/Limits
nodes.summary: John walks through the Azure cost calculator (capped estimate ~$700/month with 30% buffer, Databricks as the dominant cost) and reads through assumptions and fixed-fee limits: one medical domain, up to two cases, no model fine-tuning, no compliance certification, no production app integration. Laura confirms their MLOps engineer will review the infrastructure choices.

nodes.label: Pricing Discussion, Scope Revision, and Close
nodes.summary: John reveals the full service cost of ~35,000 pounds reduced to 13,000 pounds net after Microsoft incentives across two funding streams (Data POV and AI POV). Brandon indicates Medbrief had expected full sponsorship and may prefer to resource internally; John offers to produce a revised zero-cost-to-client proposal by removing the comparison layer and leaning on Medbrief's team. The meeting closes with thanks and a five-business-day timeline for the revised proposal.

nodes.label: Current Environment & Pain Points

provenance.quote:
CURRENT ENVIRONMENT & PAIN POINTS
Medbrief is building a platform to support medical-legal case intelligence by comparing patient case documentation against NICE medical guidelines. Today, this is a highly manual process involving legal and clinical review of large volumes of PDF documentation, requiring complex interpretation and evidence review.
KEY CHALLENGES
Large volumes of unstructured medical-legal PDF documentation.
Manual comparison between case documentation and NICE guidelines.
Need to identify correct historical NICE versions based on case dates.
Sensitive medical and personal information risks.
Entity ambiguity across patients, doctors, hospitals, and documents.
OCR limitations and inconsistent document quality.
Need for explainable, auditable, and defensible outputs.

nodes.label: Step 5: Case vs NICE Guideline Comparison

provenance.quote:
STEP 5: CASE VS NICE GUIDELINE COMPARISON

Logic & Reasoning
• Logic: Retrieve the applicable NICE guideline version based on the case date (Current or 2020).
• Comparison: Compare extracted case facts and timeline against guideline recommendations.
• Reasoning: Use AI to identify alignment or deviations, providing evidence-backed rationale.
• Explainability: Include direct references to the source document and page number.

"Recommended surgical intervention for inguinal hernia in adults is..."

VS

PATIENT CASE FACTS
"Surgery performed on 15/06/2020 using technique X..."

ASSESSMENT RESULT: ALIGNED / DEVIATED
(Evidence: Case_Doc_01, Page 4)

nodes.label: Case vs NICE Guideline Logic

provenance.quote:
CASE VS NICE GUIDELINE LOGIC

1. IDENTIFY CONTEXT
• Select the case.
• Confirm selected medical domain.
• Extract date and timeline references.

2. SELECT VERSION
• Retrieve applicable guideline by topic/date.
• Compare current vs historical availability.

3. EXTRACT FACTS
• Identify clinical events.

CASE VS NICE GUIDELINE LOGIC

1. IDENTIFY CONTEXT
• Select the case.
• Confirm selected medical domain.
• Extract date and timeline references.

2. SELECT VERSION
• Retrieve applicable guideline by topic/date.
• Compare current vs historical availability.

3. EXTRACT FACTS
• Identify clinical events.
• Identify entities (Patient, Doctor, etc.).
• Extract evidence from documents.

4. COMPARE
• What did guideline expect?
• What does case show?
• Identify alignment or deviation.

5. OUTPUT
• Assessment result and reasoning.
• Evidence references.
• Traceability to page.

nodes.label: Assumptions and Limits 1/2

provenance.quote:
ASSUMPTIONS AND LIMITS 1/2

POV SCOPE
• Limited to one selected medical domain category.
• One current and one 2020 historical NICE version.
• Process up to two selected surgery cases of the same medical domain.
• Validate technical feasibility, not production-ready.

CASE DATA SCOPE
• Digitalised, OCR-readable documents only.
• Handwritten notes and low-quality scans are Out of Scope.
• DICOM and radiology imaging are Out of Scope.

NICE API SCOPE
• Medbrief leads access, credentials, and licensing.
• Kumulus supports technical validation of access.
• Access delays may impact timeline or scope.
• Full catalog ingestion is Out of Scope.
